contentHtml:
When Energy Practices Become Spiritual Bypassing

What happens when the very tools meant to liberate your energy become the most sophisticated cages you’ve ever built? When the breathwork that promised to connect you to your body just leaves you feeling more dissociated, and the tantric practices that were supposed to ignite your intimacy become another way to perform and avoid the raw, messy truth of human connection?

The Gilded Cage of Energetic Perfectionism

We are sold a particular fantasy in the world of modern spirituality. It’s a vision of ourselves as pristine energetic beings, masters of our own vibration, capable of clearing our chakras with the same ease as we clear our email inboxes. We’re told that with enough practice, enough visualization, enough meticulously curated energy work, we can transcend the muck of our human experience. We can become untouchable, unshakeable, and perpetually in a state of high-vibrational bliss. But what if this pursuit of energetic purity is actually a profound form of self-abandonment? What if, in our desperate attempt to feel only light, we are exiling the most vital, most human parts of ourselves?

This is the seductive trap of spiritual bypassing, and it finds a particularly fertile breeding ground in the realm of energy practices. The very language of energy work—clearing, cleansing, purifying, raising—can subtly reinforce the belief that our messy, chaotic, and often contradictory inner worlds are something to be fixed or transcended, rather than met with radical acceptance. We start to see our anger, our grief, our fear not as sacred messengers, but as energetic blockages to be cleared. We use our meditations not to become more present to the full spectrum of our experience, but to float above it in a cloud of detached equanimity. I've seen people talk about 'boundaries' like they were handing out court orders. They use the language of energy protection not to cultivate a deeper sense of self-possession, but to build walls against the perceived threat of other people’s messy humanity. They speak of “cutting cords” with a casual violence that belies a deep-seated fear of authentic connection, with all its entanglements and obligations. The energy practice, which should be a gateway to deeper embodiment, becomes a fortress against the felt sense of life itself.

When ‘Clearing Your Energy’ Is a Euphemism for ‘I Can’t Handle This’

Let’s be honest. How many times have you heard—or said—some version of “I need to clear my energy” after a difficult conversation or an uncomfortable interaction? On the surface, it sounds like a responsible act of energetic hygiene. But if we look closer, what is often happening is a subtle act of avoidance. Instead of staying with the discomfort, instead of feeling the sting of the interaction in our bodies, instead of metabolizing the emotional residue of the experience, we rush to the energetic equivalent of a shower. We want to wash it all away, to return to a state of pristine neutrality as quickly as possible. We use our energy practices as a way to bypass the necessary and often painful work of emotional integration.

In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen this pattern dozens of times. A client will describe a fight with their partner, and then immediately launch into a description of the elaborate ritual they performed to “clear the negative energy” from their home. They’ll talk about smudging with sage, or doing a cord-cutting visualization, or spending an hour in a salt bath. And when I ask them what it was like to feel the anger, or the hurt, or the fear in their body during the fight, they will often draw a blank. They have become so adept at managing their energy that they have forgotten how to simply be with their emotions. They have traded the raw, visceral, and ultimately transformative power of feeling for the clean, controlled, and ultimately sterile experience of energetic management. This is a profound loss. Because the truth is, our emotions are not energetic toxins to be cleared. They are the lifeblood of our humanity. They are the source of our deepest wisdom, our most profound connections, and our most authentic power. When we bypass them in the name of energetic purity, we are cutting ourselves off from the very source of our own vitality. We are choosing the sterile perfection of the spiritual laboratory over the messy, chaotic, and ultimately sacred ground of our own lived experience.

The body processes emotion at the speed of the body, not the mind.

The Performance of Purity and the Fear of the Flesh

There is a subtle but pervasive asceticism that can creep into modern energy practices. It’s a sanitized spirituality that fears the body’s raw, untamed nature. We become so focused on the subtle body, the chakras, the auric field, that we forget the dense, messy, glorious reality of the physical body. The body becomes less a source of wisdom and pleasure, and more a project to be managed, a potential source of contamination. We might use energy practices to avoid the vulnerability of physical touch, or to create a sense of energetic distance from our partners. We might intellectualize our sexuality, turning it into a complex system of energy exchange and tantric theory, rather than a raw, embodied experience of connection. We perform a kind of energetic purity, hoping it will protect us from the messiness of love, sex, and intimacy.

Here, the work of relationship experts like Alexandra Solomon becomes so crucial. Her emphasis on relational self-awareness calls us to look at how we show up in our partnerships, not just in our meditation practice. It asks us to get honest about the ways we use our spiritual beliefs to avoid the hard work of intimacy. Are you using your daily “energy clearing” ritual to avoid a difficult conversation with your partner about your sex life? Are you talking about “energetic incompatibility” as a way to sidestep a real conversation about mismatched desires or unresolved resentments? The body keeps the score. It knows when you are using your spirituality as a shield. It knows when you are performing purity as a way to avoid the terrifying, exhilarating, and ultimately healing work of being truly seen by another. You can have the most pristine auric field in the world, but if you can’t look your partner in the eye during sex, what have you really accomplished? A link to a deeper exploration of true intimacy can be found on my site.

From High Vibes to Deep Roots: The Myth of the Upward Spiral

The dominant metaphor in much of the New Age and modern spiritual world is one of ascent. We talk about “raising our vibration,” “ascending to higher levels of consciousness,” and moving “upward” on a spiral of evolution. But this relentless focus on upward movement is a profound betrayal of the body and the earth. It reinforces the old, patriarchal idea that spirit is good and matter is bad, that heaven is up and the body is down. It teaches us to be ashamed of our density, our messiness, our rootedness in the earth. It creates a spiritual hierarchy where the disembodied, intellectual, and “high-vibrational” is valued above the embodied, emotional, and “low-vibrational.”

But what if the path to true liberation is not up, but down? What if it’s not about transcending the body, but about descending more fully into it? What if it’s not about raising our vibration, but about expanding our capacity to feel the full spectrum of human experience, from the most exquisite joy to the most gut-wrenching grief? This is the path of embodiment. It’s the path of the Tantrika, the shaman, the earth-based mystic. It’s the path that says that our divinity is not found in some distant, ethereal realm, but right here, in the blood and bone and breath of our own bodies. It’s the path that understands that true power is not about floating above the world, but about rooting ourselves so deeply into it that we become an unshakable force of nature. The past does not exist. It is a cancelled check, a forgotten wind, a story I created to feed my ego. When we are truly rooted in the present moment, in the felt sense of our own bodies, the stories that once defined us lose their power. We stop trying to escape our history and start learning to metabolize it, to compost it into the rich soil of our own becoming. This is a different kind of power. It’s not the flashy, performative power of the spiritual influencer. It’s the quiet, immense power of a mountain, a river, a tree. It’s the power that comes from saying yes to all of it. For those struggling with this, understanding the practice of embodied presence is a foundational step.

No amount of sage is going to clear this.

The Tyranny of the ‘Good’ Emotion

Spiritual bypassing through energy work often creates a subtle hierarchy of emotions. Love, joy, gratitude, and peace are the “good” emotions, the ones that are seen as evidence of our spiritual progress. Anger, grief, fear, and shame are the “bad” emotions, the ones that are seen as evidence of our energetic blockages or our spiritual immaturity. We become emotional puritans, constantly monitoring ourselves for any sign of a “negative” feeling, and then rushing to our energetic toolkits to clear it away. We might use a loving-kindness meditation to plaster over our rage, or a gratitude practice to bypass our grief. We become so afraid of our own shadow that we create a kind of inner police state, where any “unspiritual” emotion is immediately arrested and exiled.

But this is a profound misunderstanding of the nature of emotions. Our emotions are not good or bad; they are simply information. They are the body’s way of communicating with us, of telling us what we need and what is out of alignment in our lives. Anger is the sacred fire that burns away everything that is not true. Grief is the holy water that cleanses the heart. Fear is the primal intelligence that keeps us safe. When we label these emotions as “negative” and try to get rid of them, we are not only cutting ourselves off from their wisdom, but we are also doing violence to ourselves. We are telling a part of ourselves that it is not welcome, that it is not worthy of love. If you haven't screamed into a towel, dry-heaved your oppressive lineage into the Earth, or told a spiritual mentor to fuck off while holding full love in your heart — you might still be in spiritual kindergarten. This is not a call to indulge in drama, but a call to embrace the full, raw, and often terrifying power of your own emotional body. It is a call to stop performing a sanitized version of spirituality and to start living a real one. The journey into the healing power of sacred rage is not for the faint of heart, but it is essential for true liberation.

The Body as Altar, Not Battlefield

Ultimately, the antidote to spiritual bypassing in energy practices is a radical shift in orientation. It is a shift from seeing the body as a battlefield to be conquered to seeing it as an altar to be worshipped. It is a shift from using our practices to fix ourselves to using them to know ourselves. It is a shift from seeking transcendence to embracing immanence. When we make this shift, our energy practices are no longer about clearing, cleansing, and purifying. They are about listening, honoring, and loving. Our meditation is no longer about escaping the body, but about inhabiting it more fully. Our breathwork is no longer about managing our energy, but about surrendering to the wild, untamed intelligence of the life force itself. Our tantric practices are no longer about achieving some kind of spiritual peak experience, but about finding the sacred in the most ordinary moments of connection.

We stop trying to be perfect energetic beings and we start learning to be whole human beings. We stop using our spirituality to build a fortress around our hearts and we start learning to let our hearts be broken open by the beauty and the pain of the world. We stop trying to deepen your practice and we start learning to dance with the full, messy, and glorious spectrum of life. You are not a brand. You're a cosmic bonfire dissolving into light. This is not a failure of your energy practice; it is its ultimate fulfillment. It is the moment when you stop using your spirituality to escape your humanity and you start using it to become more fully, more wildly, more unapologetically human. This is the path of embodied awakening. It is the path of sacred intimacy. It is the path of coming home to yourself. For those seeking to deepen this connection, exploring how breath is the bridge between bodies can be a powerful practice.

The opposite of numbness is not pain. It is aliveness.

Frequently Asked Questions

How can I tell if I'm using my energy practice for spiritual bypassing?

The most telling sign is a persistent pattern of using your practice to numb, avoid, or prematurely “fix” difficult emotions. If your immediate response to anger, grief, or fear is to rush to a clearing technique rather than allowing yourself to feel the emotion in your body, you may be bypassing. Other indicators include a sense of detachment from your physical body, a feeling of superiority over those who are more “emotional,” and a pattern of intellectualizing your feelings rather than truly inhabiting them. True spiritual practice increases our capacity for intimacy with all of life, not our ability to float above it.

Is it wrong to want to feel good? Are energy practices for feeling better always a form of bypassing?

Of course not. The desire to feel good is a natural human impulse. The distinction lies in the intention and the outcome. It is one thing to use a practice to ground and resource yourself so that you have the stability to face a difficult truth or feel a painful emotion. It is another thing entirely to use a practice to create a state of artificial positivity that denies the reality of your experience. The goal is not to eradicate pain, but to expand our capacity to be with it. The aim is integration, not amputation. A practice should make you more available to life, not less.

The Unfolding Path of Embodiment

This journey away from spiritual bypassing and into deep embodiment is not a one-time decision, but a moment-to-moment practice. It is the practice of choosing the real over the ideal, the messy over the clean, the felt over the conceptual. It is the practice of learning to trust the wisdom of your body, even when it speaks in whispers of discomfort or roars with inconvenient truths. It is the practice of allowing your heart to break, and trusting that it will break open, not apart. There is no finish line, no certificate of completion. There is only the path, unfolding one breath, one sensation, one vulnerable moment of connection at a time. Let the energy practices serve this unfolding, not hinder it. Let them be a way to come home to the sacred ground of your own being, not a way to escape it. You are not a project to be perfected. You are a mystery to be lived. And the body, in all its wild and holy messiness, is your greatest guide.

This site provides educational content about conscious intimacy and sacred sexuality. It is not therapy. For sexual trauma, consult a licensed somatic or sex therapist.
